openingMessage: Nobody wins alone. Nobody builds a company alone. Nobody protects a location alone. We succeed by winning the small daily battles.
leadershipThought: Assume people are trying to do the right thing. But standards do not move. You can hold both.
opsContent:
Thursday Carry-Forward Status (2026-06-24):
• Alex Clubb — Christi (vessel return): Back on-site today to tackle sludge and gases from bulk separator vessel. Supplied air required from arrival — do not enter vessel area without respiratory protection confirmed. H2S peaked at 97 ppm off the bleeder Wednesday. Air monitoring continuous throughout.
• Michael Hutt — SM Coonskin pad: Job extended through June 29. Elevated H2S carry-forward. Confirm monitoring plan and crew communication protocol before any site visit.
• Ralph Forbes — University 6-48A #101H: Still awaiting Exxon job status confirmation as of Wednesday EOD. Do not mobilize until update received from Exxon.
• Dennis Easley / Larry Taylor — SM Energy Gipple: Gipple 134 rig maintenance ongoing (down since 06-23). Gipple 124H H2S run with Dennis ongoing. Both locations need current escalation and monitoring plan confirmed before crew on-site.

Notes going into Thursday:
• Heat is a live hazard — build hydration stops into your LMRA before the job starts, not after someone feels it.
• Any adjacent wellhead activity (SimOps) that was not in your original job plan requires a crew re-brief before proceeding.
exxonMoments:
Casing-in-Hole Adaptation — Hensley P29J (Line of Fire)
Crew was running casing in the hole — rig floor was off-limits. Established a 45 ft buffer zone in case casing dropped. Used the stand-down time to walk and document every sensor and muster point on location with Justin. Brought the lesson back: extra supplies (tape, batteries, mask bags, tags, sensors) need to be pre-staged before arriving on a rig location.

SWA and Unsafe Conditions — Tracy B #1 (Operational Discipline)
Unsafe conditions identified on arrival and communicated to PIC before work began. Stop Work Authority exercised. Full controls confirmed — LOTO, atmospheric monitoring, respiratory protection, exclusion zones, hold points. Left site safe. Nathan Contreras, 06/22.

Heat Triggers Noted — University 3-19 702H (Operational Discipline)
Adjacent active wellheads flagged as SimOps trigger. Heat exhaustion listed as a recognized hazard on a multi-location day. Lesson documented: be more prepared for weather conditions — build it into the LMRA. Raymundo Solis, 06/22.

Full Controls Confirmed — Oliver 39 (Atmospheric Monitoring)
Atmospheric hazards tested prior to first break. LOTO, exclusion zones, and hold points all confirmed in place. Crew verified field conditions rather than relying on paperwork. Job left with atmosphere confirmed safe. Justin Fontenot, Big Lake, 06/22.

Unsafe Conditions Flagged and Controlled — Donovan 2U 121 (Operational Discipline)
Unsafe conditions identified before work began. All controls confirmed before crew proceeded — LOTO, atmospheric monitoring, respiratory protection, exclusion zones, hold points, LMRA. Crew verified actual field conditions. Job site left safe. Nathan Contreras, 06/22.

fieldNews.headline: Cindy Smith — Hensley P29J
fieldNews.detail: Crew was running casing in the hole — no access to the rig floor. Instead of waiting, Cindy and Justin used the stand-down to walk every sensor and muster point on location and photograph them for documentation. Then she came back with a concrete lesson: pre-stage tape, batteries, mask bags, tags, and backup sensors before arriving on a rig job. That is turning a constraint into a capability.

fieldNews.headline: Nathan Contreras — Two Jobs, Full Controls Both Times
fieldNews.detail: Tracy B #1 in the morning (SWA exercised, unsafe conditions communicated to PIC, all controls confirmed) and Donovan 2U 121 in the afternoon (same standard, site left safe). Two different locations, same discipline. That consistency across a full day is what the standard looks like.

fieldNews.headline: Raymundo Solis — Honest Lesson Documented
fieldNews.detail: On a two-location day at University 3-19, Raymundo identified active adjacent wellheads as a SimOps trigger point and flagged heat exhaustion as a live hazard. Lesson written: be more prepared for weather conditions. Self-reporting what went wrong on a job that otherwise went well — that is the kind of learning that prevents incidents.

todaysChallenge: Before you speak on the next job — ask yourself: What outcome am I trying to create?
todaysChallengeSubtext: If you can't answer that, don't speak yet. Outcome-driven communication solves problems. Emotion-driven communication creates them.
